title: Chat with MES: LLM-driven user interface for manipulating garment manufacturing system through natural language

agents.description: Request Re-writer agent that preprocesses user inputs by identifying and disambiguating named entities using a vector database of registered entities from the production database, replacing ambiguous references with standardized entity names.

Multi-step Dynamic Operations Planner that decomposes complex user requests into a chain of basic executable operations (SQL, Tool invocations, Thought steps) with parameter placeholders, using few-shot learning to incorporate production guidelines.
Response Generator that consolidates intermediate results from all executed operations and produces a coherent textual response in markdown format for the user.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: The CWM system uses a sequential orchestration graph where user input flows through three stages: (1) Request Re-writing for entity disambiguation, (2) Multi-step Dynamic Operations Planning and Execution where a planner decomposes the task and executes SQL, Tool, and Thought operations step-by-step with dynamic parameter retrieval, and (3) Response Generation that summarizes all intermediate results. The LangChain framework coordinates tool invocations.

multi_agent_coordination_strategy.multi_agent_coordination_strategy_descri: Three specialized agents operate in a linear pipeline: the Request Re-writer preprocesses input, the Operations Planner decomposes and executes multi-step operations, and the Response Generator produces the final output. Each agent passes its output to the next in sequence.

human_integration.human_integration_description: Workers and managers interact with the CWM system through a conversational text interface (Dify chatbot page) using natural language. The system is human-initiated and designed to replace the traditional GUI. The paper discusses a proposed confirmation step where users review planned operations before execution, assigning responsibility to the user upon confirmation. The system may ask clarifying questions when entity information cannot be matched.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Garment Manufacturing Execution System (MES) control and manipulation, covering order management, production task assignment, material allocation, inventory management, and production planning in garment factories.
retrieval.problem_description: Traditional GUI-based MES interfaces impose high development and maintenance costs on developers and significant learning costs and operational complexity on workers. Replacing GUI with LLM-based natural language interfaces introduces challenges: (1) ambiguous and non-standardized user inputs leading to incorrect database operations, and (2) complex multi-step manufacturing tasks requiring precise, sequential database operations and tool invocations that current LLMs cannot reliably handle.
retrieval.proposed_solution: Chat with MES (CWM): an LLM agent system integrating GPT-4o into MES as a natural language interface. Two key innovations: (1) Request Re-writer that uses a vector database of registered entities and RAG to disambiguate and standardize user inputs; (2) Multi-step Dynamic Operations Planning and Execution that pre-plans a chain of SQL, Tool, and Thought operations with few-shot learning to incorporate production guidelines, then executes them step-by-step with dynamic parameter retrieval.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: A simulated garment MES with a relational database of 16 tables and 312 records was developed. A benchmark dataset of 55 manually designed requests covering common MES operations was created. CWM was evaluated against baseline models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o as Text2SQL) using execution accuracy as the primary metric. Ablation studies assessed the contribution of each component (Request Re-writer, Multi-step Operations, Tool operations, Thought operations) and the impact of different foundation LLMs.

decision_quality_improvement: CWM achieves 80% execution accuracy vs. GPT-4o baseline at 60% (20% improvement)
CWM achieves 80% execution accuracy vs. GPT-3.5 baseline at 25.5%
Request Re-writer contributes approximately 9.1% accuracy improvement over CWM without re-writer (70.9%)
Multi-step Dynamic Operations Planning and Execution contributes approximately 18.2% accuracy improvement over CWM without multi-step operations (61.8%)
Removing Tool operations reduces accuracy from 80% to 65%
Removing Thought operations reduces accuracy from 80% to 67%
CWM with GPT-3.5 achieves only 16% accuracy, lower than GPT-3.5 alone (25.5%)
reliability_metrics: CWM execution accuracy: 80% on 55 manually designed requests

ce_technique_effectiveness.retrieval-augmented-generation: Request Re-writer using RAG for entity disambiguation contributes ~9.1% accuracy improvement by eliminating Information Deviation errors
ce_technique_effectiveness.dynamic-decomposition: Multi-step Dynamic Operations Planning and Execution contributes ~18.2% accuracy improvement by reducing Wrong CoT and Wrong Syntax errors
ce_technique_effectiveness.chain-of-thought: Thought operations within multi-step planning enable mathematical calculations and complex reasoning; removing them reduces accuracy from 80% to 67%
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Few-shot learning with 3 representative examples injects production guidelines and database schema knowledge into the planner

failure_modes: Information Deviation: LLM fails to identify correct entity information from user input (e.g., 'PolyU' vs 'Hong Kong Polytechnic University')
Wrong CoT: LLM fails to comprehend user intention or constructs incorrect logical chain
Wrong Syntax: Syntax errors in generated SQL commands or tool invocation statements
Else: Other errors such as mathematical calculation errors
GPT-3.5 based CWM fails to generate syntactically correct SQL commands
limitations: Accuracy improvement through more precise workflows or additional few-shot examples requires significant manpower and cannot be easily generalized to other MES systems
Complex workflows and lengthy prompts lead to high LLM token consumption (CWM uses ~436K tokens vs ~311K for GPT-4o baseline)
Responsibility for incorrect operations by the LLM agent is unclear; proposed confirmation step not yet implemented
Free interaction mode may lead users to provide insufficient information, requiring repeated clarification
System is still far from applicable in real factory settings and replacing traditional GUI interfaces
CWM with weaker foundation models (GPT-3.5) performs worse than the model alone, indicating strong dependency on model capability

baseline_methods: CWM without Multi-Step Dynamic Operations Generation
identified_research_gaps: Balancing execution accuracy, generality, and token usage — improving accuracy via more precise workflows or few-shot examples requires significant manpower and cannot be easily generalized to other MES systems
Determining responsibility for incorrect operations performed by the LLM agent, including incorporating confirmation functions and counterfactual inference

Designing an optimal human-AI chatting interface that is universally applicable, human-centered, and suitable for manufacturing environments, accounting for different stakeholder roles (customers, production managers, workers)
Evolving CWM into a more extensible and dynamic framework that can understand database schema and production guidelines at lower cost
Reducing high LLM token consumption caused by complex workflows and lengthy prompts
